🇨🇴 Lulo Bank partners with Mercado Libre

Colombian digital bank Lulo Bank and Mercado Libre have announced a partnership allowing Lulo credit card holders to split purchases on the marketplace into 2, 3, 6, 9 or up to 12 interest-free installments in selected  stores.

The agreement targets one of the main barriers to digital credit adoption in Colombia, where e-commerce buyers still lack access to affordable online financing. 

The deal is part of Lulo Bank’s broader strategy to position its credit card as a daily-use tool with benefits  across categories including technology, travel, wellness and fashion.

$MELI The profitability fear is overplayed

A lot of the bear case is basically: “competition will force higher marketing spend, and MELI will lose unit economics.”

But when you actually do the math, the opposite shows up.

If you approximate CAC payback as marketing spend divided by incremental gross profit (a rough but useful sanity check), it improved from ~13.2 months (2024) to ~11.8 months (2025).

Translation: MELI’s customer acquisition machine became more efficient, not less.
In a tougher environment.
That’s not what a company under structural pressure looks like.

¡Qué tierno, Mashi! Un vendedor voluntario te regala rosas ecuatorianas para la tumba del santo Mujica, el tipo que "regalaba" su sueldo mientras su estado seguía exprimiendo a todos con impuestos y regulaciones.

Voluntad pura, ¿ves? Eso es mercado libre en acción: caridad genuina sin coerción. Mientras tanto, tu "revolución ciudadana" dejó a Ecuador endeudado hasta el cuello, represión a indígenas y más estatismo fallido.

Mujica y vos adorando la austeridad socialista, pero el verdadero milagro es cómo el (parcial) capitalismo crea riqueza para que hasta izquierdistas compren flores. 

El socialismo del siglo XXI ya mató millones; el mercado sigue vivo y enterrando vuestras utopías. 🪦💸

¡Sigue llorando sobre tumbas, que la propiedad privada y la libertad individual te pagan las rosas!

$MELI recently shared how much money moves through its platform in Latin America. In 2024, they helped drive R$381 billion in Brazil, $15 billion in Mexico, and $62 billion in Argentina.

The platform also supported millions of small businesses and helped create over 140k jobs across the region.

The data is mostly meant to show local governments that MercadoLibre is good for their economies. There is one major takeaway for investors. The data shows that most of these small businesses rely on MercadoLibre for their main income and use Mercado Pago as their primary financial service or bank. This means sellers are completely locked into the ecosystem. Because businesses depend on the platform to survive, MercadoLibre has the power to raise its fees over time without losing its customers.

$MELI is executing a deliberate strategy to dominate Latin America under Szarfsztejn's leadership. They are actively trading immediate margins for total ecosystem control. Let's take a look at each region.

In Argentina, they use their near-monopoly to test new business models at a very low cost. The region serves as an incubator for high-frequency product innovation. They test complex logistics concepts here before exporting them to more contested regions.

In Brazil, MercadoLibre is aggressively entering the grocery and pharmacy markets to force daily app usage. To bypass strict government regulations, they outright acquired a Pharmacy in São Paulo in late 2025. This forces them into a direct, capital-intensive retail model temporarily just to grab immediate market share. For groceries, they are avoiding the high costs of dark stores by leveraging strategic partnerships to handle inventory while they control the actual delivery.

In Mexico, the strategy is built entirely on speed to beat traditional omnichannel retailers like Walmart. MercadoLibre recently opened a massive new cross-docking facility called XEM3 in Cuautitlan Izcalli. This 80k sq meter hub currently processes up to 600k packages every single day. They are actively replacing traditional long-term storage with rapid, immediate transit to guarantee same-day delivery across major cities.

This entire physical system directly feeds their fintech operations. By tracking daily, high-frequency purchases like medicine and food, MercadoLibre gathers highly granular consumer data. This allows Mercado Credito to use AI algorithms to issue loans to unbanked populations with incredibly low default rates. The heavy capital expenditures required to build this logistics network are then directly subsidized by their highly profitable and rapidly growing retail media business.

Let's take a look at the 2026 Mexican ecom landscape. It is a structural war between $MELI and $AMZN. Let's breakdown how MercadoLibre is beating Amazon's global playbook in a high-stakes duopoly.

Mexico is no longer an emerging market. It is a digital retail powerhouse. Market models project the TAM could reach US$177B in 2026. This explosive growth is heavily driven by expanding 5G networks and the rapid digitization of unbanked citizens. However, standard global retail strategies fail in this unique environment.

Amazon operates at a severe, foundational disadvantage. Its entire business model relies on high banking penetration and ubiquitous credit card usage. In Mexico, roughly half the adult population remains unbanked or underbanked. Amazon lacks a native financial ecosystem and relies on clunky 3P workarounds for cash payments. This friction results in extremely low Amazon Prime adoption, forcing the company to burn massive capital on local logistics without the recurring revenue buffer it enjoys in the US. To compensate for this cash drain, Amazon was forced to institute aggressive fee increases on 3P sellers in early 2026.

MercadoLibre took the exact opposite approach and built a localized monopoly. Instead of waiting for traditional banks to digitize the population, they built the digital infrastructure themselves through Mercado Pago. Mercado Pago established a massive decentralized network that allows users to instantly digitize physical cash at neighborhood stores. This ecosystem completely bypasses traditional banking hurdles and effectively captures the informal cash economy.

The ultimate weapon in this war is Mercado Pago's pursuit of a Multiple Banking Institution license. Securing this license will drastically lower their cost of capital by allowing them to legally capture massive pools of retail deposits. They use the high-margin profits from this financial arm to heavily subsidize cheaper retail shipping, structurally undercutting Amazon.

The financial technology sector in Mexico is highly volatile and capital-intensive. MercadoLibre is successfully outspending local challengers to build a monopolistic digital infrastructure. The most credible threat to this duopoly is government intervention. The Federal Economic Competition Commission deeply investigated both platforms. The antitrust watchdog confirmed they hold immense market share and create barriers to entry. However, aggressive antitrust actions risk explicitly violating the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement ahead of its mandatory 2026 review. Legally crippling Amazon and MercadoLibre would likely backfire, handing the consumer market to highly subsidized Chinese platforms like Temu and Shein.

The strategic reality is heavily skewed. Amazon relies entirely on brute-force capital deployment. MercadoLibre wins through deep, systemic integration with the Mexican cash economy. MercadoLibre's localized financial flywheel makes it nearly the unassailable force in Latin American digital commerce.
